title: Master Summary: French
content:
### Definite & Indefinite Articles
Definite: le (m), la (f), l' (vowel), les (pl). Indefinite: un (m), une (f), des (pl). Gender is crucial for all nouns.

---

### Essential Verbs (Être & Avoir)
Être (to be) and Avoir (to have) are the most important verbs. They are used as auxiliaries for compound tenses.

---

### Verb Groups (-er, -ir, -re)
Regular verbs follow patterns based on their endings. -er verbs are the largest group (e.g., parler, manger).

---

### Passé Composé
Used for completed actions in the past. Formed with auxiliary (avoir/être) + past participle. Watch for 'Dr. Mrs. Vandertramp' verbs for être.

---

### Adjective Agreement
Adjectives must match the gender and number of the noun they describe. Usually, add -e for feminine and -s for plural.

---

### Negation (Ne... pas)
Sandwich the conjugated verb between 'ne' and 'pas'. Example: 'Je ne mange pas' (I do not eat).

---

### Question Formation
Three ways: Intonation (rising voice), 'Est-ce que...', or Inversion (e.g., Parles-tu?).

---

### Basic Greetings
Bonjour (hello), Salut (hi/bye), Merci (thanks), S'il vous plaît (please), Enchanté (nice to meet you).

---

### Pronunciation (Silent Letters)
Final consonants (d, s, t, x) are usually silent unless followed by a word starting with a vowel (Liaison).

---

### Partitive Articles
Used for unspecified quantities: du (m), de la (f), de l' (vowel). Usually translated as 'some'.

---

### Future Proche
Used for actions that will happen soon. Formed with conjugated 'aller' (to go) + infinitive. Example: 'Je vais manger' (I am going to eat).

title: Master Summary: German
content:
### The Four Cases
Nominative (subject), Accusative (direct object), Dative (indirect object), and Genitive (possession). Case affects articles and endings.

---

### Three Genders
Der (masculine), Die (feminine), Das (neuter). Noun gender is often arbitrary and must be learned with the noun.

---

### Verb Position (V2 Rule)
In a main clause, the conjugated verb is always the second element. The subject can move, but the verb stays at position 2.

---

### Plural Nouns
Plurals are varied (add -e, -er, -en, -s, or umlaut). There is no single rule, but all plurals use 'die' in the nominative.

---

### Modal Verbs
Können (can), Müssen (must), Sollen (should), Wollen (want), Dürfen (may), Mögen (like). They change word order.

---

### Separable Verbs
Prefixes that separate and move to the end of the sentence (e.g., 'aufstehen' -> 'Ich stehe um 8 Uhr auf').

---

### Personal Pronouns
Ich, du, er/sie/es, wir, ihr, sie, Sie (formal). Note the difference between informal 'du' and formal 'Sie'.

---

### Basic Greetings
Guten Tag (hello), Auf Wiedersehen (goodbye), Danke (thanks), Bitte (please/welcome), Entschuldigung (excuse me).

---

### Compound Nouns
German loves joining nouns to create new words. The gender of the compound is determined by the last noun (e.g., der Haustürschlüssel).

---

### Perfekt Tense
Spoken past tense. Formed with 'haben' or 'sein' + past participle (usually starting with ge- and ending at the end of the sentence).

---

### Prepositions with Dative
Certain prepositions always take the dative case: aus, bei, mit, nach, seit, von, zu. Memorizing these is essential.

title: Master Summary: Japanese
content:
### Writing Systems
Three scripts: Hiragana (phonetic, native words), Katakana (phonetic, foreign words), and Kanji (logographic, Chinese characters).

---

### Particles (Wa & Ga)
Wa (は) marks the topic (what we are talking about). Ga (が) marks the specific subject (the doer of the action).

---

### Particles (Ni, De, Wo)
Wo (を) marks direct object. Ni (に) marks destination/time. De (で) marks location of action/method.

---

### Desu & Masu (Politeness)
Basic polite forms. 'Desu' for nouns/adjectives, 'Masu' for verbs. Crucial for social interactions.

---

### Verb Conjugation (Groups)
Group 1 (U-verbs), Group 2 (Ru-verbs), and 2 Irregulars (Suru, Kuru). Conjugation depends on the final syllable.

---

### Adjectives (i vs na)
i-adjectives (end in -i, conjugate like verbs) and na-adjectives (behave like nouns, need 'na' before nouns).

---

### Sentence Structure (SOV)
Subject-Object-Verb. The verb always comes last. Subject is often omitted if understood from context.

---

### Basic Greetings
Konnichiwa (hello), Arigatou (thanks), Sumimasen (excuse me/sorry), Sayounara (goodbye), Ohayou (morning).

---

### Numbers & Counters
Japanese uses specific 'counters' depending on the object's shape/type (e.g., -mai for flat things, -hon for long things).

---

### Te-form Verbs
Used for connecting sentences, requesting (te kudasai), or ongoing actions (te iru). Foundational for intermediate grammar.

---

### Potential Form (Can do)
Used to express ability. Group 1: change final -u to -eru. Group 2: change -ru to -rare-ru. Irregulars: Suru -> Dekiru, Kuru -> Korareru.

title: Master Summary: Chinese
content:
### The Four Tones
Mandarin has 4 tones: Flat, Rising, Dipping, Falling. A change in tone changes the word's meaning (e.g., ma).

---

### Pinyin System
The official system to transcribe Chinese characters into Latin script. Crucial for learning pronunciation and typing.

---

### Sentence Structure (SVO)
Basic order is similar to English: Subject + Verb + Object. Time and location usually come before the verb.

---

### Measure Words (Measure Particles)
Mandarin requires measure words between a number and a noun. 'Ge' (个) is the most common general measure word.

---

### Negation (Bu & Mei)
'Bu' (不) is used for present/future. 'Mei' (没) is used to negate 'you' (to have) and past actions.

---

### Particles (Ma, Le, De)
'Ma' (吗) for questions, 'Le' (了) for change of state/completion, 'De' (的) for possession or description.

---

### Basic Greetings
Ni hao (hello), Xie xie (thanks), Zai jian (goodbye), Dui bu qi (sorry), Mei guan xi (no problem).

---

### Pronouns
Wo (I), Ni (you), Ta (he/she/it). Add 'men' (们) for plural (e.g., women = we).

---

### Numbers 1-10
Yi (1), Er (2), San (3), Si (4), Wu (5), Liu (6), Qi (7), Ba (8), Jiu (9), Shi (10). Logical system for larger numbers.

---

### Verbs (No Conjugation)
Chinese verbs never change form for tense or person. Tense is indicated by context or specific particles like 'le'.

---

### Comparative Structures (Bi)
Use 'Bi' (比) to compare two things. Structure: A + Bi + B + Adjective. Example: 'Ta bi wo gao' (He is taller than me).

title: Master Summary: Korean
content:
### Hangeul (Alphabet)
A scientific writing system consisting of 24 basic letters (14 consonants, 10 vowels) arranged in blocks.

---

### Subject & Topic Particles
Un/Neun (은/는) for topics. I/Ga (이/가) for subjects. Correct usage is key to sounding natural.

---

### Object Particle (Eul/Reul)
Eul/Reul (을/를) marks the direct object of a sentence. It follows the noun receiving the action.

---

### Sentence Structure (SOV)
Similar to Japanese: Subject-Object-Verb. The verb always comes last and carries the tense/politeness markers.

---

### Honorifics & Politeness
Speech levels are built into the language. Common endings: -nida (formal), -yo (polite/standard), banmal (informal).

---

### Basic Greetings
Annyeong-haseyo (hello), Gamsa-hamnida (thanks), Joesong-hamnida (sorry), Ssaranghae (I love you - informal).

---

### Numbers (Sino vs. Native)
Two systems: Native Korean (for age, hours, counting) and Sino-Korean (for dates, money, minutes, phone numbers).

---

### Verbs & Adjectives
Verbs and adjectives behave similarly (descriptive verbs). All end in '-da' in dictionary form.

---

### Question Words
Nugu (who), Mueot (what), Eodi (where), Eonje (when), Wae (why), Eotteoke (how).

---

### Past Tense Formation
Usually involves adding -at/eot- (았/었) to the verb stem before the final ending.

---

### Connecting Sentences (-go)
Use '-go' (고) to connect two actions or states. It's like 'and' in English. Example: 'Meokgo masyeoyo' (I eat and drink).

title: Master Summary: Pali
content:
### Language of Theravada
An Indo-Aryan language used in the Buddhist scriptures (Tipitaka). Closely related to Sanskrit but structurally simpler.

---

### Connection to Thai
Thai vocabulary is heavily influenced by Pali, especially in religion, law, and academic terms (e.g., Sathana, Metta).

---

### Noun Inflection (Declension)
Nouns change endings based on 8 cases, 3 genders, and 2 numbers. Similar to Latin or Sanskrit.

---

### The 8 Cases
Nominative, Vocative, Accusative, Instrumental, Ablative, Dative, Genitive, Locative. Each indicates a different role.

---

### Verb Conjugation
Verbs are conjugated for person (1st, 2nd, 3rd) and number (singular, plural). Tenses include Present, Aorist, Future.

---

### Sandhi (Euphony)
The process of sounds blending at word boundaries. Essential for chanting and reading original texts correctly.

---

### Essential Vocabulary
Dhamma (truth/law), Sangha (community), Buddha (enlightened one), Kamma (action), Nibban (extinction of suffering).

---

### Tipiṭaka Structure
Vinaya (discipline), Sutta (discourses), Abhidhamma (philosophy). All written in Pali.

---

### Three Genders
Masculine (Pullinga), Feminine (Ithilinga), Neuter (Napumsakalinga). Gender affects the endings of nouns and adjectives.

---

### Roots (Dhatu)
Words are built from 'roots'. For example, the root 'Bhū' (to be) gives 'Bhavati' (he is) and 'Bhava' (becoming).

---

### Numbers 1-10
Eka (1), Dvi (2), Ti (3), Catu (4), Pañca (5), Cha (6), Satta (7), Attha (8), Nava (9), Dasa (10). Similar to Sanskrit numbers.

title: Master Summary: Spanish
content:
### Gender of Nouns
Nouns ending in -o are usually masculine (el). Nouns ending in -a are usually feminine (la). There are exceptions like 'el mapa'.

---

### Verb Conjugation (-ar, -er, -ir)
Three regular verb groups. Conjugation changes the ending to match the subject (e.g., Yo hablo, Tú hablas).

---

### Ser vs. Estar (To Be)
'Ser' for permanent traits (identity, origin). 'Estar' for temporary states (location, mood). Crucial distinction.

---

### Subject Pronouns
Yo, tú, él/ella/usted, nosotros, vosotros, ellos/ellas/ustedes. Subject pronouns are often omitted as the verb ending shows the subject.

---

### Basic Greetings
Hola (hello), Gracias (thanks), Por favor (please), De nada (you're welcome), Adiós (goodbye).

---

### Plural Formation
Add -s to words ending in a vowel. Add -es to words ending in a consonant (e.g., libro -> libros, ciudad -> ciudades).

---

### Questions & Negation
Use 'no' before the verb for negation. Questions use inverted marks (¿?) and often just change intonation.

---

### Numbers 1-10
Uno, dos, tres, cuatro, cinco, seis, siete, ocho, nueve, diez. Spelling changes in 11-20.

---

### Possession (De)
Spanish doesn't use 's. Use 'de' (of). Example: 'El perro de Juan' (Juan's dog).

---

### Adjective Placement
Adjectives usually come AFTER the noun they describe (e.g., el gato negro). They must agree in gender and number.

---

### Reflexive Verbs
Used when the subject and object are the same. Need reflexive pronouns: me, te, se, nos, os, se. Example: 'Me llamo' (I call myself).
